slides.title: Strings Have Built-In Tools
slides.body: Every string in Python comes with a built-in **toolkit of methods** — helpers you call with a dot. They let you transform, search, and clean text without writing any complex code yourself.
slides.tip: A method is just a function that lives on the string: `"hello".upper()`

slides.title: Change Case in One Call
slides.body: Use `.upper()`, `.lower()`, and `.title()` to control capitalization. Perfect for displaying names nicely or comparing text fairly.

slides.title: Methods Return NEW Strings
slides.body: Calling a method **never changes the original string** — it hands you back a brand-new one. Always save the result in a variable, or the change is lost.
slides.code:
word = "python"
word.upper()        # result thrown away!
print(word)         # still lowercase
big = word.upper()
print(big)
slides.tip: Forgetting to save the result is the #1 beginner mistake with string methods.

slides.title: Clean, Find, Split, Join
slides.body: `.strip()` removes extra spaces, `.replace(old, new)` swaps text, `.split()` breaks a string into a list, and `.join()` glues a list back together.

slides.title: Chain Methods for Power Combos
slides.body: Because each method returns a new string, you can **chain** them — calling one right after another on a single line to transform text step by step.
slides.code:
raw = "  Ali Khan  "
username = raw.strip().lower().replace(" ", "_")
print(username)
slides.tip: Read chains left to right: strip first, then lower, then replace.

slides.title: You've Got the Toolkit!
slides.body: String methods are some of the most-used tools in Python. Master `.strip()`, `.lower()`, `.replace()`, `.split()`, and `.join()` and you can handle almost any real-world text challenge.
slides.tip: All string methods are non-destructive — the original is always safe.
